name: healthcare-ai-deploy
topic: A healthcare AI startup (Series B, 18 months runway) has a diagnostic model for detecting diabetic retinopathy from retinal images. The model achieves 87% accuracy, matching average radiologist performance. Should they pursue FDA clearance and deploy now with human-in-the-loop, or invest 12-18 months to reach 95% accuracy before seeking clearance?
key_questions: Is 87% accuracy sufficient given that it matches current standard of care (average radiologist)?
What does the FDA clearance pathway look like at 87% vs 95%, and does higher accuracy meaningfully change the regulatory burden?
What are the data and compute requirements to go from 87% to 95%, and is 12-18 months realistic?
Can the company survive 12-18 months of continued burn without revenue from deployment?

expert_checklist.item: Comparison to standard of care, not to 100% — 87% matching average radiologist IS clinically meaningful
Sensitivity vs specificity trade-off — false negatives (missed disease) vs false positives (unnecessary referrals)
FDA regulatory pathway — 510(k) vs De Novo classification, predicate device comparison
Clinical validation study design — prospective vs retrospective, sample size, multi-site
Post-market surveillance requirements and continuous monitoring obligations
Real-world vs lab performance gap — model accuracy degrades on different cameras, lighting, populations
Training data bias — demographic, geographic, device diversity in training set
Data requirements for 87% to 95% — diminishing returns curve, likely needs 5-10x more data
Competitor timeline and first-mover advantage in AI diagnostics
Reimbursement pathway — CPT codes, payer acceptance, willingness to pay for AI-assisted reads
Liability and malpractice implications — who is responsible when AI-assisted diagnosis is wrong?
Human-in-the-loop workflow design — alert fatigue, override rates, radiologist acceptance
Runway and business survival — 18 months runway vs 12-18 months delay means near-zero margin for error

traps.description: Claiming 87% is insufficient without comparing to standard of care — the benchmark is average radiologist, not perfection
Advocating 'ship now, improve later' without acknowledging that medical device modifications require new regulatory submissions (not a software update)
Assuming 87% to 95% is a simple training improvement without addressing the exponential data requirements and diminishing returns
